title: Weekly synthesis: LAN, WAN, OSI Model
objective: Lock in the week's material, surface weak spots, and tidy your notes.
week_focus: Networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies
checkpoint: Can describe how data moves through a simple network.
resource_names: Cisco CCST Networking / Network Technician path
plain_language_intro: Today reviews the week's main concepts as one connected model.
mini_lecture_markdown:
This is the review day for Networking foundations: models, media, devices, and topologies. The goal is to connect the week's main ideas into one working model rather than introduce a new topic. The core terms are LAN, WAN, OSI Model, TCP/IP Model, Network Media, and Network Interface Card.

Across the week, the key mechanisms were: LAN: uses local switching and addressing so devices exchange frames directly across a contained network segment WAN: uses routed links across carrier or internet paths so separate local networks can exchange traffic over distance OSI Model: breaks communication into layers so encapsulation, addressing, transport, and applications can be analyzed one function at a time TCP/IP Model: organizes communication into link, internet, transport, and application roles so packet flow can be traced across the stack The value of the review is in seeing how these pieces connect across setup, operation, failure, and troubleshooting.

For CompTIA Network+ (N10-009), this block is most useful when you can separate similar concepts, place them at the right layer, and connect them to symptoms or evidence. That usually means following a chain from cause to behavior to visible result.

At this point, the week should read as one sequence rather than separate definitions. Each concept should connect to a role, a mechanism, and a likely scenario where it matters.

One useful review lens is to track how the week's concepts relate to symptoms, evidence, and next-step decisions in a short scenario.

The main point of the review is to keep the concepts connected rather than treat them as isolated terms.
exam_relevance_markdown: Expect this material to show up as scenario-style reasoning rather than raw recall. Typical tasks include distinguishing related concepts, choosing the right component or control, identifying the most likely cause from symptoms, and explaining why one workflow step belongs before another. If you can reconstruct the week without the notes open, you are closer to the level these exams reward.
key_distinctions: LAN vs WAN — LAN: Local area network that connects devices across a limited physical space such as a room, office, or campus. WAN: Wide area network that connects distant sites across provider infrastructure or large geographic areas.

WAN vs LAN — WAN: Wide area network that connects distant sites across provider infrastructure or large geographic areas. LAN: Local area network that connects devices across a limited physical space such as a room, office, or campus.

OSI Model vs TCP/IP Model — OSI Model: Seven-layer reference model used to describe how network communication functions. TCP/IP Model: Practical layered model used to describe how internet protocols work together.

TCP/IP Model vs OSI Model — TCP/IP Model: Practical layered model used to describe how internet protocols work together. OSI Model: Seven-layer reference model used to describe how network communication functions.

Network Media vs Copper Cabling — Network Media: Physical transmission medium that carries network signals, such as copper, fiber, or radio. Copper Cabling: Network cabling medium that carries data as electrical signals over metal conductors.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. LAN: uses local switching and addressing so devices exchange frames directly across a contained network segment WAN: uses routed links across carrier or internet paths so separate local networks can exchange traffic over distance OSI Model: breaks communication into layers so encapsulation, addressing, transport, and applications can be analyzed one function at a time TCP/IP Model: organizes communication into link, internet, transport, and application roles so packet flow can be traced across the stack That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
self_check_prompts: Explain the difference between LAN and WAN without using the words "better" or "faster" as your whole answer.
Describe what LAN does, what input it works on, and what evidence would show that it is functioning correctly.
Walk through today's objective in sequence and say which step would fail first if the system were misconfigured.
Connect today's glossary terms to this week's deliverable: what part of the artifact proves that you understand the mechanism and not just the label?
Rebuild the week from memory in under three minutes and note which transition between concepts is still weak.
quick_recap: This review day consolidates LAN, WAN, and OSI Model into one connected model.
